caption: Figure 1. Morphology and type of inclusions in 1 # sample. a–e) single particle inclusions and f–i) composite inclusions.
paper_abstract: Three experiments of Fe-15Mn-xAl-C (x = 1, 3, 5) low-density steel with different Al contents are carried out, and the characteristics of inclusions are observed through field-emission scanning electron microscopy. The results show that the types of inclusions in the three samples are almost the same, mainly $\mathsf { A l } _ { 2 } \mathsf { O } _ { 3 } , \mathsf { A l N } ,$ MnS, TiN, ${ \sf A l } _ { 2 } { \sf O } _ { 3 }$ -MnS, AlN-MnS, TiN-MnS, and ${ \sf A l } _ { 2 } { \sf O } _ { 3 }$ -AlN-MnS. The number percentage of ${ \sf A l } _ { 2 } { \sf O } _ { 3 }$ decreases, while the number percentage of AlN increases with the increase of Al content in the three samples. As the Al content increases, the number of inclusions between 1 and 5 μm decreases, while the number between 5 and 10 μm increases. FactSage8.2 software calculation shows that an increase in Al content leads to an increase in the liquidus and a decrease in the solidus of the steel; at the same time, it causes the austenite region to narrow and expands the ferrite region. The change in the solidification route leads to a delay in the solidification fraction of AlN, MnS, and TiN inclusions precipitation. At last, the 2D mismatch theory shows that the mismatch degrees of $\mathbf { \Delta A l _ { 2 } O _ { 3 } } / \mathbf { M n S } ,$ , AlN/MnS, TiN/MnS, and $\mathsf { A l N } / \mathsf { A l } _ { 2 } \mathsf { O } _ { 3 }$ are 8.41%, 8.94%, 11.65%, and 11.66%, respectively.
paper_title: Effect of Al Content on Inclusions in Fe-15Mn-xAl-C Low-Density Steel
citation_sentences: shows the morphology and type of inclusions in 1 # Figure 1sample. Figure 1a shows an elongated ${ \mathrm { A l } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 3 }$ inclusion with a length higher than 8 μm. Figure 1b shows an approximately elliptical AlN inclusion. Figure 1c shows the extracted elongated AlN inclusion with a size of ≈12 μm. Figure 1d shows a single-particle MnS inclusion, and Figure 1e shows an angular TiN inclusion. Figure 1f shows ${ \mathrm { A l } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 3 }$ -MnS composite inclusion in the inner and outer layers. Figure 1g shows the element surface distribution of AlN-MnS inclusions. Figure 1h indicates the TiN-MnS composite inclusion, and it can be observed that there is a clear interfacial relationship between MnS and TiN inclusions. Figure 1i shows the 3D morphology of $\mathrm { A l _ { 2 } O _ { 3 } { \cdot } A l N { \cdot } M n S }$ inclusions. By scanning the elemental surface distribution in Figure 1i, the composition of the composite inclusions can be clearly observed.

citation_sentences: shows the size distribution of inclusions in 1 # to 3 # Figure 5samples. From Figure 5, it can be observed that the number of inclusions with sizes between 1 and 5 μm accounts for 58.4%, 53.6%, and 48.2% of the total number in 1 # to 3 # samples, respectively. The number of inclusions with sizes between 5 and 10 μm accounts for 32.3%, 34.6%, and 46.9% of the total number in 1 # to 3 # samples, respectively. The number of inclusions with sizes higher than 10 μm accounts for 9.4%, 11.8%, and 4.9% of the total number in 1 # to 3 # samples, respectively. In 1 # to 3 # samples, the number of inclusions with sizes ranging from 1 to 5 μm decreases with increasing Al content, the number of inclusions with sizes ranging from 5 to 10 μm increases with increasing Al content, and the number of inclusions with sizes higher than 10 μm shows a trend of first increasing and then decreasing with increasing Al content.

citation_sentences: To investigate the effect of Al content on the solidification route of three samples, the phase diagram module in FactSage 8.2 was used to calculate the Fe–C–Si–Mn–P–S–Al–O–N–Ti system and the calculation results are shown in . shows the Figure 6 Table 3phase composition of the corresponding numbers in Figure 6. It can be seen that the solidification route of 1 # sample is: $\mathrm { L } \longrightarrow \mathrm { L } + \mathrm { F C C } \longrightarrow \mathrm { F C C } _ { : }$ , while the solidification route of 2 # and 3 # samples is: $\mathrm { L \longrightarrow L + B C C \longrightarrow L + B C C + F C C \longrightarrow L + F C C \longrightarrow }$ FCC and $\operatorname { L } \to \operatorname { L } + \operatorname { B C C } \to \operatorname { L } + \operatorname { B C C } + \operatorname { F C C } \to \operatorname { B C C } + \operatorname { F C C } \to \operatorname { F C C } ,$ respectively. The increasing content of Al leads to the solidification route of 2 # and 3 # samples passing through the BCC phase, and the area where the FCC phase exists decreases.
Table 3. The phase composition corresponds to the number in Figure 6.

citation_sentences: different degrees of segregation of Al and N during the solidification stages. From , it can be seen that the degree of Figure 8segregation of N element is much higher than that of Al element. Therefore, when there is no significant difference in N content in the steel, the increasing in content of Al promotes the precipitation of AlN inclusions. In 3 # sample, the solubility of N significantly decreased, leading to a delay in the solidification fraction of AlN inclusion precipitation. Consequently, the solidification fraction of AlN inclusion precipitation is delayed as a result of the combined action of the Al and N elements.

The number percentage of ${ \sf A l } _ { 2 } { \sf O } _ { 3 }$ decreases, while the number percentage of AlN increases with the increase of Al content in the three samples. As the Al content increases, the number of inclusions between 1 and 5 μm decreases, while the number between 5 and 10 μm increases. FactSage8.2 software calculation shows that an increase in Al content leads to an increase in the liquidus and a decrease in the solidus of the steel; at the same time, it causes the austenite region to narrow and expands the ferrite region. The change in the solidification route leads to a delay in the solidification fraction of AlN, MnS, and TiN inclusions precipitation. At last, the 2D mismatch theory shows that the mismatch degrees of $\mathbf { \Delta A l _ { 2 } O _ { 3 } } / \mathbf { M n S } ,$ , AlN/MnS, TiN/MnS, and $\mathsf { A l N } / \mathsf { A l } _ { 2 } \mathsf { O } _ { 3 }$ are 8.41%, 8.94%, 11.65%, and 11.66%, respectively.
paper_title: Effect of Al Content on Inclusions in Fe-15Mn-xAl-C Low-Density Steel
citation_sentences: Table 7shows the formation process of inclusions in three Figure 12steels during smelting and solidification. A large number of single-particle ${ \mathrm { A l } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 3 }$ inclusions are formed during the smelting process when the Al content is 1%. With the increasing content of Al, the precipitation amount of single-particle ${ \mathrm { A l } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 3 }$ inclusions in the 3% and 5% samples decreases. In the subsequent solidification process, AlN, MnS, and TiN inclusions begin to precipitate, in the order of $\mathrm { A l N } > \mathrm { M n S } > \mathrm { T i N }$ . The solidification fraction of AlN inclusion precipitation is advanced with the Al content increased from 1% to 3%, and the solidification fraction of AlN inclusion precipitation is delayed with the Al content increased from 3% to 5%. The solidification fraction of MnS and TiN inclusions precipitated is delayed with the increasing content of Al. In solid steel, the first precipitated inclusions can provide nucleation sites for the later precipitated inclusions. Therefore, $\mathsf { A l } _ { 2 } \mathsf { O } _ { 3 } { \cdot } \mathsf { M } \mathsf { n } \mathsf { S } _ { \mathrm { \Omega } }$ , AlN-MnS, and TiN-MnS composite inclusions were observed in solid steel. Due to the presence of relatively small amounts of single-particle ${ \mathrm { A l } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 3 }$ inclusions in samples containing 5% Al, no $\mathrm { A l } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 } – \mathrm { A l N } – \mathrm { M n S }$ inclusions were observed in the steel.
